data.title: Crude oil extends losses as Trump appears to signal approval of Iran ceasefire deal
data.description:
Crude oil futures add to their declines Thursday on expectations that an agreement to open the Strait of Hormuz could come anytime soon, as President Trump said he is making a "final determination" on a preliminary deal to extend the ceasefire with Iran.

"I will be meeting now, in the Situation Room, to make a final determination," Trump said in a Truth Social post this morning that also reiterated previous statements that Iran will never have a nuclear weapon and that the strait must be open and any mines destroyed or removed.

Underscoring that tensions remain, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, the speaker of Iran's parliament, said earlier in his own social media post that "we have no trust in guarantees or words, the only criterion is action," and that "the winner of any agreement is the one who is better prepared for war the day after."

Following Trump's post, Iranian media reportedly rejected the president's claims about a deal, calling his comments "a mixture of truth and lies."

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for July delivery down 2.1% to $87.08/bbl, and front-month Brent (CO1:COM) July crude down 1.9% to $91.91/bbl.

Crude prices have dropped more than 17% this month as the market awaits some sort of deal between the U.S. and Iran.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (XLE)

data.title: EasyJet jumps on report of takeover interrest
data.description:
EasyJet (EJTTF) rose 5.5% in London trading amid some takeover speculation.

There's speculation that the takeover interest is coming from a private equity firm working with an aircraft leasing company, according to a Betaville "uncooked" alert that was circulating among traders on Friday.

data.title: SEC proposes to rescind Biden-era climate-related disclosure rules
data.description:
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission proposed on Friday to undo a rule adopted during the Biden administration that required companies to disclose certain climate-related information in their registration statements and annual reports.

The move will return the agency's focus to its core mandate, "restoring a materiality-focused approach to securities regulation," the commission said in a statement.

"SEC disclosure obligations should comply with the commission’s statutory authority, be guided by materiality as the North Star, avoid the practical effect of dictating corporate behavior, and be imposed only when the expected benefits justify the likely costs and burdens," SEC Chairman Paul S. Atkins said in a statement.

With the adoption of the rule in 2024, "We exceeded our authority," Atkins said in an interview on Fox Business's "Mornings with Maria" program on Friday. The rescission, he said, is part of the SEC's efforts to "get back to first principles."

He is working to make the process of going public simpler and to modernize it. "A lot of rules we have are based on 1930s, 1940s thinking," Atkins said.

The climate effects disclosure requirement was approved in March 2024 and mandated "highly specific and granular disclosure from virtually all public companies about climate-related matters," the SEC said. Those included such things as greenhouse gas emission, management of climate-related risks, and the financial statement effects of severe weather effects.

Since then, the requirements have hit some legal obstacles. In April 2024, the SEC stayed the rules pending the completion of consolidated litigation in the U.S. Court of Appeals. In March 2025, the Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit issued an order putting the petitions on hold while the SEC reconsidered the challenged rules by notice-and-comment rulemaking or renewed its defense of the climate disclosure rules. 

The SEC now says it wants to rescind the rules "in their entirety," saying the agency had exceeded its authority. Its reasons for rolling them back include:

  • The rules are "unnecessary and inconsistent with a registrant-specific, materiality-based approach."
  • They "stray well beyond the policy concerns of the federal securities laws."
  • The requirements "impose substantial costs on public companies and their shareholders that are not justified by the informational benefits they may provide to some investors."
  • They are "at odds with the commission’s policy objectives of facilitating capital formation and promoting public company status."

The SEC will keep the public comment period open for 60 days after the Federal Register publishes the proposed rescission.

data.title: Blackstone to sell Seattle office building for ~54% below purchase price - report
data.description:
Blackstone (BX) is selling the U.S. Bank Center in Seattle to the office landlord Spear Street Capital for ~$280M, about 54% below the building's 2019 purchase price of $612M, people with knowledge of the deal told Bloomberg News.

The deal, which is contingent on the availability of financing, is reportedly illustrative of the persistence of pandemic-era decline in office demand, compounded by higher borrowing costs in the following years.

The world's largest alternative asset manager told the media house that U.S. traditional office represents less than 1.5% of their portfolio, and it was effectively written off in 2023.

Seattle is among areas that have performed the worst, with a third of downtown offices still vacant at March-end, the report noted, citing CBRE Group (CBRE). However, the region more broadly has seen rising demand. Especially, east of Seattle has seen large tenants and AI technology companies adding office space.

Blackstone is also said to have shifted its attention to east of Seattle.

data.title: Trump’s Hormuz 'deal sheet' drops: No nukes, no tolls
data.description:
Iran must agree never to develop a nuclear weapon and immediately reopen the Strait of Hormuz to unrestricted shipping traffic, President Donald Trump said, outlining what he described as terms under consideration for a possible arrangement with Tehran.

“The Hormuz Strait must be immediately open, no tolls, for unrestricted shipping traffic, in both directions,” Trump wrote on Truth Social, adding that the U.S. naval blockade around Iran “will now be lifted.”

Trump said ships stranded in the strait due to the blockade could begin “heading home” and claimed U.S. forces had already destroyed numerous naval mines through underwater operations. He added that Iran would complete the removal or detonation of any remaining mines in the waterway.

The president also described what he said would be a coordinated effort involving the United States, Iran, and the International Atomic Energy Agency to recover and destroy enriched nuclear material buried underground following U.S. B-2 bomber strikes 11 months ago.

“The enriched material, sometimes referred to as ‘Nuclear Dust,’” would be unearthed and destroyed “in close coordination and conjunction with the Islamic Republic of Iran,” Trump wrote.

Trump added that “no money will be exchanged until further notice" and said “other items, of far less importance, have been agreed to,” without elaborating further.

He said he was heading into the White House Situation Room to make a “final determination,” suggesting discussions over a potential arrangement with Iran remained ongoing.

The post comes a day after reports suggested that the U.S. and Iran had reportedly reached a deal, but Trump will have to give the final approval.

data.title: UiPath reports 'good' Q1 results, but analysts also mull growth acceleration
data.description:
Shares of UiPath (PATH) rose about 1% on Friday after “good” fiscal first quarter results, but analysts also discussed growth acceleration trajectory.

Oppenheimer maintained its Perform rating with no price target after the company reported “good” fiscal first quarter results.

Analysts led by Brian Schwartz said that, positively, the results display solid net-new Annual Recurring Revenue, or ARR, and operating margin growth, and the company's management issued strong commentary on the agentic AI platform and large Systems Integrator, or SI, partner momentum.

"Negatively, the F1Q revenue beat is low quality, gross margin is compressing from AI investments, and F2Q net-new ARR guidance shows little growth. While management raised the FY27 outlook above the F1Q upside, the outlook also implies decelerating growth across key metrics as y/y comparisons get harder. Although F1Q results show good execution, it is unlikely to shift investor opinions on the name nor mitigate concerns that intensifying competition in the agentic era from the mega IT suppliers will weaken UiPath's moat and seat-based model," said Schwartz and his team.

BofA reiterated its Underperform rating with a $13 price objective.

Analysts led by Koji Ikeda said UiPath reported “good” first quarter fiscal 2027 results above BofA/Street estimates and raised the full year outlook. Net-new ARR, the key building block for driving accelerating ARR growth revenue growth, declined 32% quarter-over-quarter to $48M (was +78% year-over-year on an easy compare), which is making a sustained accelerating ARR growth path less certain, according to the analysts.

The analysts added that the quarterly results were likely good enough given its discount multiple to its infrastructure software peers. "However, we look for more data points that support a sustainable ARR growth acceleration trajectory before turning more positive. Update forecast for better subscription and professional services growth assumptions," said Ikeda and his team.

RBC Capital Markets kept its Sector Perform rating and $12 price target.

"UiPath reported a beat and raise quarter to start the FY with solid performance across key metrics. We were encouraged by AI-driven deal momentum and improved customer retention as we watch for signs of NNARR stabilization and potential acceleration. FY/27 guidance was raised as management feels positive about underlying business trends, pipeline health, a more stable demand environment, and customer momentum," said analyst Matthew Hedberg and his team.

Update: The story was updated with stock price movement.

data.title: KNOP outlines dropdown pursuit over next 4 to 5 years while preparing for $220M refinancing in September 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KNOT Offshore Partners (KNOP) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Revenues were $92 million, operating income was $14.7 million. Net income was $2.6 million. Adjusted EBITDA was $56.5 million" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • "As of March 31, 2026, we had $140.7 million in available liquidity, made up of $92.7 million in cash and cash equivalents plus $48 million in undrawn capacity" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • "Following the end of the quarter, we declared a cash distribution of $0.05 per common unit" and management framed this as the start of a higher payout phase: "We are pleased to have initiated the process of increasing the distribution after an extended period of low payouts" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • "From January 1, 2026, we changed the useful life estimates of our vessels from 23 years to 20 years" and management emphasized income statement impact rather than cash impact: "This will increase future depreciation quarter-by-quarter, but that is not a cash item" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • Management highlighted charter actions and new coverage, including: "We exercised our option to continue the time charter of Hilda Knutsen with Shell through March 2027" and "subsequently agreed a new time charter with Eni commencing in Q3 2027 for 3 years fixed plus options up to a further 3 years" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • Additional commercial updates included: "TotalEnergies exercised their option to extend the charter of Anna Knutsen for 1 year until May 2027" and "we agreed to time charter for Recife Knutsen with Transpetro to commence in Q3 2026 for a fixed period of 2 years" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • On market conditions and coverage, management said: "In both Brazil and the North Sea, we continue to see tightening markets driven by FPSO start-ups, ramp-ups, expansions and new developments" and added: "We have sustained a strong backlog with $858 million of fixed contracts, averaging 2.4 years" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

Outlook

  • Management did not provide explicit EPS or revenue guidance for upcoming quarters in the prepared remarks or Q&A.

  • On distribution trajectory, management reiterated process rather than targets: "That's something that gets decided on by the Board each time they make a dividend or distribution declaration" and "we don't have specific guidance on numbers that may be coming" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • On fleet growth, management linked future distribution capacity to dropdowns and rechartering: "we anticipate pursuing these acquisitions over the next 4 to 5 years to the extent that relevant terms are attractive and are approved by our Conflicts Committee" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

Financial results

  • Management’s quarter figures were: "Revenues were $92 million" and "Adjusted EBITDA was $56.5 million" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • Liquidity was described as rising versus year-end: "This available liquidity was $3.7 million higher than at December 31" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • Utilization was presented with and without dry-dock effects: "We operated the 97.2% utilization taking into account scheduled dry docking" and "92% utilization overall following the dry dockings of Tuva Knutsen and Bodil Knutsen" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • On financing cost context, management said: "the average margin on our floating rate debt during the first quarter was 2.22% over SOFR" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

Q&A

  • Fredrik Dybwad, Fearnley Securities AS: asked how investors should think about "modest gradual increase" in distributions and the likely magnitude; CEO & CFO Lowe responded, "we don't have specific guidance on numbers" and reiterated the process: "the Board will make their decision following the end of second quarter for the next distribution."

  • Liam Burke, B. Riley Securities: asked whether geopolitical disruptions could drive higher offshore development even after the Strait of Hormuz reopens; CEO & CFO Lowe said, "I don't particularly have a view over the medium to longer term" and added, "I don't really have a view on whether it would change what will otherwise happen in the medium to long term."

  • Charles Fratt, Alliance Global Partners: asked about the "sequential decline in revenues" and whether it was downtime or charter rollovers; CEO & CFO Lowe answered, "it will relate to the dry dock schedule" and also "the terms of the contracts that are outstanding between different period."

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts): questions pressed on distribution pace and specificity ("in terms of magnitude") and probed revenue decline drivers, while also raising macro/geopolitical uncertainty.

  • Neutral (management): prepared remarks emphasized "tightening markets" and backlog, but Q&A responses were bounded by limited visibility and board discretion, including "we don't have specific guidance" and "I don't particularly have a view."

  • Versus last quarter, management again avoided committing to capital allocation formulas; the distribution discussion shifted from “under active review” in Q4 to reporting an actual increase to $0.05 in Q1, while analysts continued to press for clearer payout and capital allocation priorities.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The cash distribution rose to "$0.05 per common unit" in Q1 from "$0.026 per common unit" discussed for Q4 (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • Backlog was described as "$858 million of fixed contracts" in Q1 versus "$929 million of fixed contracts" as of December 31, 2025 in the Q4 call (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • The sponsor’s unsolicited offer was a prominent topic in Q4 ("offer...to buy...for $10 per common units"), while Q1 discussion centered on charter extensions, dropdown runway, and the distribution increase (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

Risks and concerns

  • Refinancing remains a focal point: management said it is "look ahead to a $220 million facility in September 2026 and the $65 million facility in October" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • Operational downtime risk was evident in the quarter’s revenue discussion: "it will relate to the dry dock schedule" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

  • Market visibility limits were acknowledged in response to macro questions: "I don't particularly have a view over the medium to longer term" (CEO & CFO Derek Lowe).

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q1 2026 as a quarter of solid operating performance and liquidity, paired with incremental commercial wins (extensions and new charters) and an initial step up in cash distributions to $0.05 per unit. Looking ahead, management pointed to a tightening shuttle tanker market in Brazil and the North Sea, a stated plan to pursue sponsor dropdown acquisitions over the next 4 to 5 years subject to approvals, and upcoming September/October 2026 refinancing milestones, while declining to provide numeric guidance on the pace of future distribution increases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: JPMorgan will fight CLARITY Act in current form, Jamie Dimon says
data.description:
Jamie Dimon vowed that JPMorgan Chase (JPM) will fight the stablecoin legislation known as the CLARITY Act in its current form, he said in an interview aired on Friday.

"We're not worried," he said about the proposed legislation that, in its current version, would allow crypto platforms to pay interest-like rewards on stablecoins. "We want it to be fair," Dimon said in an interview on Fox Business' "Mornings with Maria" program.

The current draft of the CLARITY Act allows fintechs to "effectively pay interest on stablecoins without the protections," he said. "This will be fought."

 "No one is going to bow down to this guy, okay, or that company," he said, referring to Coinbase Global (COIN) and its CEO, Brian Armstrong.

Specifically, if crypto companies are offering bank-like products, they should be regulated like a bank, he said. "They need to follow the same rules," he said. “It should be fair and equal. Period.”

The bank chief is still wary about the use of stablecoin as a broad payment structure. "I do think it will be used for cross-border payments, for small-dollar payments, for a person-to-person type of thing," Dimon said.

For wholesale payments, the use of stablecoin requires costs for converting the money to stablecoin and then back into money. "Remember, you have to buy the stablecoin and sell the stablecoin," he said. "So, there are transaction costs on both sides."

On a broader scale, he sees the value in distributed ledger technology. "We do blockchain. It’s a legitimate technology," he said.

data.title: Sensata downgraded to Equal Weight after recent share price gains: Barclays
data.description:
Sensata Technologies (ST) was downgraded to Equal Weight from Overweight by Barclays due in part to its recent rally.

The share price of the Massachusetts-based producer of sensors for various industries has surged nearly 50% year to date.

"Much of the appreciation in the share price, we believe, has been due to enthusiasm regarding emerging data center exposure at ST, but this is not likely to contribute meaningfully to revenue until mid-2027," said Barclays analysts in a Friday note. "Near-term muted auto production (~60% of sales) is a headwind despite LSD% outperformance. We also note that a long-standing activist shareholder exited ST in Q1."

Barclays set a $53 price target on Sensata.

Earlier this month, Truist Securities upgraded Sensata to Buy from Hold and raised its price target to $58 from $43 due to its growing exposure to AI and data center infrastructure.

data.title: Global rate cuts still lead hikes, but momentum is fading - BofA
data.description:
Central banks around the world continue to ease monetary policy this year, though the pace of rate cuts is losing steam as inflation concerns linger.

According to BofA Global Research, there have been 31 rate cuts versus just 12 hikes year to date, indicating that easing still leads the global monetary landscape.

However, the balance is starting to shift. On a three-month rolling basis, developed markets have moved back toward neutral, suggesting the bar for additional cuts is rising.

The data indicate the previous disinflation phase that made rate cuts straightforward is over, as the energy (USO) (BNO) (UNG) (BOIL) shock from the Middle East conflict drives up producer and consumer prices. Central banks remain net easers, but with far less conviction than before, and some are already being pulled toward more hawkish positioning amid persistent energy concerns.

Emerging markets (EEM) remain further along in the easing cycle than their developed counterparts (VEA), but even there, the trend appears less one-sided than it did just months ago.
